deals_cleared.merger_description: Gilead Sciences, Inc. (Gilead), through its wholly owned subsidiary Ravens Sub, Inc. (Ravens Sub) proposes to acquire all the outstanding shares of Arcellx, Inc. (Arcellx) via a public cash tender offer. Following consummation of the offer, Ravens Sub will be merged with Arcellx, and Arcellx will continue as the surviving corporation and a wholly owned subsidiary of Gilead.

events.title: Gilead - Arcellx - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Gilead Sciences, Inc. (Gilead), through its wholly owned subsidiary
Ravens Sub, Inc. (Ravens Sub) proposes to acquire all the
outstanding shares of Arcellx, Inc. (Arcellx) via a public cash tender
offer. Following consummation of the offer, Ravens Sub will be
merged with Arcellx, and Arcellx will continue as the surviving
corporation and a wholly owned subsidiary of Gilead.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Gilead, is a biopharmaceutical company incorporated
in the United States with headquarters in Foster City, California.
Globally, Gilead’s activities focus on advancing medicines to prevent
and treat life-threatening diseases, including HIV, liver disease,
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19), inflammation and cancer. Its
primary subsidiary in Australia is Gilead Sciences Pty. Ltd., through
which Gilead supplies Australian customers with a range of
medicines, including, but not limited to cell therapies for oncology
indications. In addition, Gilead runs clinical trials in Australia and
employs an operations team to support those trials.
Arcellx, the target, is a clinical-stage biotechnology company
incorporated in the United States, with headquarters in Redwood
City, California. It is focused on developing immunotherapies for
patients with cancer and other incurable diseases.
Arcellx’s operations are limited to the United States. It has not
generated revenues from the sale of products in Australia to date,
and its nexus to Australia is limited to clinical trials exclusively run
by Gilead. Its clinical and pre-clinical programs focus on products for
the treatment of cancer indications, primarily multiple myeloma,
which are co-developed and co-commercialised by Gilead and
Arcellx through an existing agreement. Outside the United States,
Gilead has the exclusive right to apply for regulatory approval,
manufacture and commercialise any anito-cel products developed
as cancer treatments, including for indications resulting from other
clinical trials.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
There is no existing horizontal overlap between Gilead and Arcellx in
Australia.
As described above, there is an existing relationship between Gilead
and Arcellx allowing Gilead to exclusively commercialise jointly
developed products in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There are no competitive overlaps between Gilead and Arcellx in
the research, development or marketing of multiple myeloma
cell therapies in Australia.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Maas - 50% acquisition of COLAS’ Caloundra asphalt plant and 100% of COLAS’ Tomago asphalt plant - Phase 1 Determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Maas Group Holdings Limited (Maas) proposed acquisition, through
subsidiary Asphalt Operators Australia Pty Ltd (AOA), of 50% interest
in Coltek Asphalt Pty Ltd (Coltek), which will own and operate the
hot-mix asphalt business currently owned and operated by a
subsidiary of COLAS Australia Group Pty Ltd (COLAS) at Caloundra
on the Sunshine Coast, Queensland and, through its subsidiary
Austek Roads NSW Pty Ltd (Austek Roads NSW), of 100% interest in
COLAS’ hot-mix asphalt business at Tomago, NSW (Newcastle).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Maas, is a diversified industrial group with operations
in construction materials, civil construction and hire, manufacturing
and equipment sales, and residential and commercial real estate.
Under the Austek brand the AOA business produces asphalt and
supplies roadlaying services (such as asphalt paving, asphalt repairs
and spray seal services).
The targets are two hot-mix asphalt plants owned by COLAS, in
Caloundra and Tomago. COLAS supplies asphalt, road maintenance
and preservation services, quarrying and binder management
services, dust management and quality control services, and
research and development services. COLAS is a subsidiary of
France-based COLAS SA.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Maas and COLAS both supply hot-mix asphalt production and road
laying services, however the parties do not currently compete in the
same geographic markets.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and information from third partes.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• For the supply of hot-mix asphalt and roadlaying services,
there is no horizontal overlap between Maas and COLAS in
in the Newcastle/Hunter region. In the Sunshine Coast
region there may be some minimal overlap.
• The Parties would likely continue to be constrained in both
regions by alternative suppliers of hot-mix asphalt and
roadlaying services including Boral, Downer EDI and Fulton
Hogan.
• The Parties are unlikely to have the ability and/or incentive
post-acquisition to foreclose downstream rivals’ access to
hot-mix asphalt as there are alternative ex-bin hot-mix
asphalt suppliers including Boral, Downer EDI and Fulton
Hogan.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Yancoal Australia Limited (**Yancoal**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Kestrel Coal Group Pty Ltd (**Kestrel Coal Group**) from investment funds and/or their investors which are managed by EMR Capital Management Limited or EMR Capital Advisors Pty Ltd and Adaro Capital Limited (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Yancoal Australia Limited (Yancoal) applied for a notification waiver
in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the shares in
Kestrel Coal Group Pty Ltd (Kestrel Coal Group) from investment
funds and/or their investors which are managed by EMR Capital
Management Limited, EMR Capital Advisors Pty Ltd, and/or Adaro
Capital Limited, as described in the transaction documents provided
as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
13 April 2026

The acquirer, Yancoal, produces thermal coal and, to a lesser extent,
metallurgical coal, from mines located in New South Wales,
Queensland, and Western Australia.
The target, Kestrel Coal Group, holds an 80% interest in the Kestrel
Joint Venture, an unincorporated joint venture which owns and
operates the Kestrel Coal Mine in Queensland. The remaining 20% of
the Kestrel Coal Joint Venture is held by Mitsui Kestrel Coal
Investment Pty Ltd.
The Kestrel Coal Mine is located in Queensland’s Bowen Basin and
produces metallurgical coal, and to a lesser extent, thermal coal. The
Acquisition would result in Yancoal holding an 80% interest in the
Kestrel Coal Mine.
Both Yancoal and the Kestrel Coal Mine supply metallurgical and
thermal coal from mines in Australia, with the majority of supply
being exported to the global seaborne coal market.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small overlap in coal production by the parties in
Australia as Yancoal primarily produces thermal coal and
the Kestrel Coal Mine primarily produces metallurgical coal,
and both parties are primarily exporters
b. the parties’ combined share of supply of thermal and
metallurgical coal in Australia is low and the increase in
share of supply arising from the Acquisition would be small
c. there are alternative suppliers of both thermal and
metallurgical coal in Australia and globally.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: Lighthouse Service Partners Holdco Pty Ltd, through its subsidiary Lighthouse Service Partners Bidco Pty Ltd (together, **Lighthouse**), proposes to acquire 100% of the issued share capital in Precise Plumbing Pty Ltd (**Precise Plumbing**), SA Pipe Relining Pty Ltd (**SAPR**) and Precise Trade Services Pty Ltd (**PTS**) (together, **Precise Services Group**) from its current shareholders.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Lighthouse Service Partners Holdco Pty Ltd, through its subsidiary
Lighthouse Service Partners Bidco Pty Ltd (together, Lighthouse)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Precise Plumbing Pty Ltd,
SA Pipe Relining Pty. Ltd. and Precise Trade Services Pty Ltd
(together, Precise Services Group), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
13 April 2026

The acquirer, Lighthouse, is a holding company with an investment
structure that focuses on acquisitions and investments in home
services businesses across plumbing, heating ventilation and air
conditioning (HVAC), electrical and related trades in Australia.
Lighthouse is ultimately owned by Alpine Investors, a private equity
firm headquartered in San Francisco, California.
As part of its investments, Lighthouse owns Ken Hall Holdings Pty
Ltd which operates in the Greater Adelaide region and predominantly
provides residential integrated building services, including plumbing,
electrical, HVAC, roofing, tiling, bathroom renovations, handyman
services, and related home maintenance services.
The target, the Precise Services Group, provides trade services to
residential and commercial customers across the Greater Adelaide
region, including plumbing, electrical, HVAC, bathroom renovations,
and handyman services.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there are alternative suppliers of plumbing, electrical, HVAC,
bathroom renovations, and handyman services in the
Greater Adelaide region
b. the parties’ combined share in the supply of each of
plumbing, electrical, HVAC, bathroom renovations, and
handyman services to customers in Greater Adelaide is low
and the increase in share of supply arising from the
Acquisition would be small.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

Wild Bush Luxury is currently held by Experience Co Limited.

events.title: Questionnaire - Intrepid Travel - Wild Bush Luxury
events.display_title: Questionnaire - Intrepid Travel - Wild Bush Luxury

events.title: Intrepid Travel - Wild Bush Luxury - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Intrepid Travel Pty Ltd (Intrepid Travel) proposes to acquire 100% of
the share capital in Wild Bush Luxury Experience Pty Ltd (Wild Bush
Luxury) (together, the Parties) together with the transfer of relevant
intellectual property rights and employees (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Intrepid Travel, is a subsidiary of Intrepid Group Pty Ltd
(Intrepid Group). Intrepid Group is a global, purpose-led experiential
travel company specialising in small group tours and travel
experiences. Intrepid Group has 31 offices around the world and
operates more than 1,000 small group adventures in 118 countries.
The target, Wild Bush Luxury, is a boutique Australian tourism
operator that manages and operates the ‘The Maria Island Walk’ (in
Tasmania), the ‘Arkaba Walk’ and ‘Arkaba Homestead’ (in South
Australia), and ‘Bamurru Plains’ (in the Northern Territory). Wild Bush
Luxury is currently held by Experience Co Limited.

Intrepid Travel and Wild Bush Luxury overlap in the supply of guided
walking experiences and/or accommodation in:
• the Flinders Ranges in South Australia
• the Top End in the Northern Territory, and
• Maria Island in Tasmania.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The Parties are not particularly close competitors in the supply of
guided walking experiences and/or accommodation in the areas
of overlap and/or surrounding geographic areas. Intrepid Travel
only offers a single-day guided walk in Maria Island as part of a
longer multi-stop tour in Tasmania, whereas Wild Bush Luxury’s
offering involves a multi-day guided Maria Island walk with
accommodation on the island. Similarly, Intrepid Travel’s guided
walks in the Flinders Ranges and Top End involve routes and
accommodation types which are different to those offered by
Wild Bush Luxury.
• The Parties would continue to face competition from multiple
alternative suppliers of guided walking experiences and/or
accommodation in each of the areas of overlap and/or
surrounding geographic areas. The Parties would also be
constrained by consumers opting for self-guided walking tours in
these areas.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: CVC Capital Partners - Marathon Asset Management - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
CVC Capital Partners plc’s (CVC Capital Partners) proposed
acquisition of 100% of the shares in Marathon Asset Management,
LP, Marathon GP Holdings I, LLC and Marathon GP Holdings II, LLC
(together, Marathon Asset Management) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

CVC Capital Partners is listed on the Euronext Amsterdam Stock
Exchange and is part of the CVC Network, a global private equity and
investment advisory firm that provides investment advice to and/or
manages investments on behalf of funds and investment vehicles
with assets under management (AuM) globally. The CVC Network
manages AuM across private equity, credit, secondaries and
infrastructure. It holds interests in a range of industries, including
energy, renewables, transportation, chemicals, utilities,
manufacturing, retail and distribution.
CVC Capital Partners has interests in:
• Etraveli Group AB, a flight online travel agency (OTA).
• Companies which provide pharmaceutical packaging,
excipients and specialty chemicals, being Syntegon
Technology GmbH, DFE Pharma GmbH & Co. KG, and
Cohizon Life Sciences Limited.
Marathon Asset Management is a New York-based global asset
manager focused on liquid (public) and private credit. In addition to
its credit asset management and advisory activities, Marathon Asset
Management-advised funds and accounts hold equity stakes in a
small number of companies and own and lease out numerous
assets.
Marathon Asset Management has an interest in:
• Auto Europe Group, a car rental OTA business.
• Kaléo Inc, a pharmaceutical manufacturer of auto-injector
drug delivery systems for epinephrine and naloxone, which
are used to treat anaphylaxis, or severe allergic reactions,
and opioid overdoses respectively.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• The Parties’ market share aggregation in the supply of credit
asset management and any potential sub-segment is estimated
to be minimal both globally and in the APAC region, and their
market shares remain low.
• In relation to the Parties’ interests in travel related companies,
non-horizontal competition concerns would be unlikely to arise
as CVC Capital Partners would not have the ability and incentive
to foreclose rivals due to the existence of competitors, including
Flight Centre Travel, Webjet, Expedia, Trip.com, Edreams,
Skyscanner, Rental Cars, Economy Bookings and Discover Cars.
• In relation to the Parties’ interests in pharmaceutical related
companies, non-horizontal competition concerns would be
unlikely to arise as there are no existing supply relationships and
there appears to be a low risk of potential foreclosure concerns
post-acquisition.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Signant Health Holding Corp (together with its subsidiaries, Signant)
proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in ActiGraph Holdings, LLC
(together with its subsidiaries, Ametris (formerly ActiGraph)), as
described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
16 April 2026

The acquirer, Signant, is a US-based evidence generation company
that provides services used in clinical trials. Signant does not have a
physical presence in Australia but provides electronic clinical
outcome assessments (eCOAs), a data collection service for clinical
trials, clinical trial randomisation and supply management (RTSM)
services, and supplementary clinical trial professional services.
Signant supplies these services to both contract research
organisations (CROs) and directly to pharmaceutical and
biotechnology companies (together referred to as Sponsors).
The target, Ametris, is a US-based provider of digital health
technology (DHT) for clinical trials. Ametris specialises in wearables
– in particular, wrist worn wearables and other sensors – which track
biovitals and other digital endpoints for patients participating in
clinical trials. Ametris does not have a physical presence in Australia
but supplies its DHT products in Australia for non-commercial
academic research.
Both Signant and Ametris supply services used to collect digital
endpoints in clinical trials.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
• there is limited overlap between the parties in the supply of
digital endpoint collection services in Australia, as their
respective offerings primarily serve different use cases for
clinical trials
• the parties each have a small Australian presence
• there are alternative suppliers of the types of digital endpoint
collection services offered by the parties in Australia
• there appears to be a low risk of foreclosure or other
conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary conduct,
or bundling or tying of services, post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_cleared.merger_description: MA Marina Fund Holdco Pty Ltd and MA Marina Fund TT Pty Ltd (together, the **Acquirers**) propose to acquire 100% of the real property and business assets (both tangible and intangible) of the Gold Coast City Marina (**Gold Coast City Marina**). events.title: Questionnaire - MA Financial - Gold Coast City Marina
events.display_title: Questionnaire - MA Financial - Gold Coast City Marina

events.title: MA Financial Group - Gold Coast City Marina - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
MA Marina Fund Holdco Pty Ltd and MA Marina Fund TT Pty Ltd’s
(together, the Acquirers)’s proposed acquisition of 100% of the real
property and business assets (both tangible and intangible) of the
Gold Coast City Marina (Gold Coast City Marina) (the Acquisition).
The Acquirers are indirectly solely controlled by funds managed by
MA Financial Group Limited (MA Financial).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The Acquirers are part of the MA Marina Fund, managed by entities
held by MA Financial, which invests in and operates marinas. The
MA Marina Fund currently operates a portfolio of 16 marinas located
along Australia’s eastern seaboard under the ‘d’Albora’ brand (MA
Marina Portfolio). The MA Marina Portfolio includes 2 marinas in
south-east Queensland – the Pacific Point Marina in Steiglitz on the
Gold Coast, and the East Coast Marina in Manly, Brisbane.
Each marina in the MA Marina Portfolio operates as an integrated
marina services facility combining vessel storage, maintenance and
associated commercial uses. Marinas in the MA Marina Portfolio
typically provide wet berthing and other ancillary marina services
including dry storage, boatyard storage, marine workshop and
industrial leasing, and marine fuel.
The target, Gold Coast City Marina, is a full-service marina and
marina industrial facility located in Coomera, Queensland. It provides
wet berthing and other ancillary marina services including dry
storage, boatyard storage, marine workshop and industrial leasing
and marine fuel.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
The Acquirers and MA Financial (together, the Parties) overlap in the
Gold Coast region in the supply of wet berthing and ancillary marina
services, including dry storage, boatyard storage, marine workshop,
marine fuel and industrial leasing (e.g. to boat builders, mechanics,
brokers), for recreational and commercial vessels.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, information from third parties, and publicly
available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• The Parties’ market share aggregation in either the supply of
wet berthing or ancillary marina services as a result of the
Acquisition is estimated to be low on any market definition;
• The Parties would continue to face competition from
multiple alternative suppliers of wet berthing and ancillary
marina services in the Gold Coast region, including The Boat
Works, Marina Mirage, Main Beach Superyacht Marina,
Southport Yacht Club, Hope Island Marina, Sanctuary Cove,
Runaway Bay Marina and Hope Harbour Marina.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
TWG Hunter Dealerships Pty Ltd (TWG) and the Trustee for White
King Corporate Unit Trust applied for a notification waiver in respect
of TWG’s proposed acquisition of:
• 95% of the share capital in Sharton Motors Pty Ltd (Sharton
Motors); and
• the associated freehold properties (currently held by N&M
Burton Pty Ltd),
as described in the transaction documents provided as part of the
application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) not to make the notification waiver
determination applied for.
14 April 2026

The acquirer, TWG, is in the business of selling new and used cars in
Australia. Within NSW, TWG operates dealerships in Albion Park,
Coffs Harbour, Lavington, Albury, Cardiff, Rutherford, North
Wollongong, Maryville, Raymond Terrace, Hamilton East, Hamilton,
Newcastle West, and Orange.
Specifically in the Newcastle and Hunter Valley region of NSW, TWG
operates several used and new car dealerships under the following
dealership groups: Klosters Motor Group, Cardiff Motor Group and
Highways Group. TWG dealerships include Ford, Honda, Hyundai,
Nissan, Suzuki, Volkswagen, BMW, Chery, Jaecoo, Mitsubishi, MINI,
Zeekr, and JAC.
The target, Sharton Motors, operates Toyota and GWM/Haval car
dealerships in the Hunter Valley region of NSW, including the
associated sales of parts and accessories, and servicing. Sharton
Motors is owned by N&M Burton Pty Ltd.
Sharton Motors Pty Ltd operates at the following sites:
• 70 Port Stephens Drive, Taylors Beach
• 104 - 110 Newcastle Street, East Maitland
• 124 Newcastle Street, East Maitland
• Union Street, East Maitland
• 83 - 87 Melbourne Street, East Maitland
• 75 Melbourne Street, East Maitland
• 91 Melbourne Street, East Maitland
• 322 Main Road, Cardiff.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application, and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, including
Sharton Motors’ revenue in Australia, the ACCC considers that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds are likely
to be met.
The ACCC is unable to reach a view that the Acquisition would not be
likely to raise competition issues in any market based on the
information provided. It considers that the Acquisition warrants
consideration through the notification process, including via further
information gathering and consultation with third parties. In
particular:
a. there is horizontal overlap between TWG and Sharton
Motors in the supply of new and used cars in the
Newcastle and Hunter Valley region of NSW, and the
extent of competition (including any inter-brand
competition) between the parties is unclear
b. TWG’s and Sharton Motors’ combined share of the
supply of new cars in areas within the Newcastle and
Hunter Valley region, including Maitland and Lake
Macquarie, may be significant
c. there is uncertainty regarding the extent to which the
parties are likely to face intra-brand and inter-brand
competition from alternative suppliers of new cars in the
Newcastle and Hunter Valley region.
Given the above, the ACCC has determined not to make the
notification waiver determination applied for.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

deals_declined.merger_description: The INNIO Group operates in the industrial equipment and energy infrastructure sector, primarily as a global manufacturer of gas power units under the Jenbacher and Waukesha brands. Its products are supplied to industries such as power generation, oil and gas (particularly for gas compression), utilities and large-scale industrial users. INNIO’s power units are used for distributed power generation, combined heat and power applications, and gas compression, serving a broad range of commercial, industrial and utility customers.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Two wholly owned subsidiaries of INNIO Holding GmbH (INNIO
Jenbacher International B.V. and INNIO Jenbacher Netherlands B.V.),
propose to acquire from Enerflex Ltd (together with all its
subsidiaries, Enerflex Group) the issued share capital in certain
Enerflex Group operating companies in Australia, Indonesia and
Thailand, which will transfer Enerflex Group’s aftermarket service
businesses in those countries to the INNIO Group (APAC
Transaction).
The Australian element of the APAC Transaction comprises the
acquisition by INNIO Jenbacher International B.V. of all of the issued
share capital in Enerflex Service Pty Limited (Enerflex Australia)
from Enerflex Australasia Holdings Pty Ltd., an indirect, wholly-
owned subsidiary of Enerflex Ltd, as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).
On completion of the Acquisition, Enerflex Australia will become an
indirect, wholly owned subsidiary of INNIO Holding GmbH and part of
the INNIO Group’s global Jenbacher/Waukesha gas compression
power units and services business.
INNIO Jenbacher International B.V., INNIO Jenbacher Netherlands
B.V., INNIO Holding GmbH and INNIO Holding subsidiaries (together,
the INNIO Group) applied for a notification waiver in respect of the
Acquisition.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(b) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) not to make the notification waiver
determination applied for.
16 April 2026

The INNIO Group operates in the industrial equipment and energy
infrastructure sector, primarily as a global manufacturer of gas
power units under the Jenbacher and Waukesha brands. Its products
are supplied to industries such as power generation, oil and gas,
utilities and large-scale industrial users. INNIO’s power units are
used for distributed power generation, combined heat and power
applications, and gas compression, serving a broad range of
commercial, industrial and utility customers.
INNIO Holding GmbH, and thereby the INNIO Group, is indirectly held,
managed and/or advised by Advent International, L.P. (Advent)
and/or its affiliates. Advent is a globally active private equity investor
based in Boston, USA.
The target, Enerflex Australia, operates in the energy infrastructure
services sector with a focus on supporting the gas industry as well
as industrial energy users. The company provides aftermarket
services such as maintenance, repair, spare parts, field services,
retrofits, and operations and maintenance for a wide variety of gas
compression and engine-power equipment.
The INNIO Group is an upstream supplier of new compression and
power generation power units. Enerflex Australia is a downstream
multi-brand aftermarket services provider for energy infrastructure
operators. The INNIO Group and Enerflex Australia also both supply
certain spare parts for gas compression power units to customers.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has considered the
information provided with the notification waiver application and had
regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Act.
Based on the information provided in the application, including the
parties’ revenue in Australia, the ACCC considers that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds would be
met.
The ACCC is unable to reach a view that the Acquisition would not be
likely to raise competition issues in any market based on the
information provided. It considers that the Acquisition warrants
consideration through the notification process, including via further
information gathering and consultation with third parties. This may
include a focus on:
a. the vertical relationship between the products and services
supplied by the INNIO Group and Enerflex Australia, their
relative market position, and the likely impact of the
Acquisition on the parties’ ability and/or incentive to
foreclose rivals
b. the extent to which the parties are likely to be constrained by
alternative suppliers of gas compression power units and
aftermarket services (including the provision of spare parts)
to customers in Australia.
Given the above, the ACCC has determined not to make the
notification waiver determination applied for.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

ongoing_phase_1.merger_description: Eagers Vic Pty Ltd (**Eagers**) is a subsidiary of Eagers Automotive Limited (ASX: APE), a publicly listed automotive retail group which owns and operates motor vehicle dealerships across Australia. Eagers Automotive Limited sells new and used motor vehicles through its dealerships, representing around 50 automotive brands. Eagers also provides related goods and services including motor vehicle service, parts and allied consumer finance. Eagers operates 12 dealerships in the Melbourne area, including in Toorak (Mercedes-Benz), Malvern (ULR Jaguar and ULR Land Rover) and Port Melbourne (Jaguar, Volvo, Volkswagen and Land Rover). Eagers does not currently have any Audi dealerships in Victoria.
